sourceRefs.fallbackText: The web framework for perfectionists with deadlines.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The web framework for perfectionists with deadlines.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Documentation version: 6.0
sourceRefs.textQuote: Documentation version: 6.0

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Let’s learn by example.
headingPath: Writing your first Django app, part 1 ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: Let’s learn by example.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Throughout this tutorial, we’ll walk you through the creation of a basic poll application.
headingPath: Writing your first Django app, part 1 ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: Throughout this tutorial, we’ll walk you through the creation of a basic poll application.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: It’ll consist of two parts:
headingPath: Writing your first Django app, part 1 ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: It’ll consist of two parts:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: A public site that lets people view polls and vote in them.
headingPath: Writing your first Django app, part 1 ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: A public site that lets people view polls and vote in them.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: An admin site that lets you add, change, and delete polls.
headingPath: Writing your first Django app, part 1 ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: An admin site that lets you add, change, and delete polls.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: We’ll assume you have Django installed already. You can tell Django is installed and which version by running the following command in a shell prompt (indicated by the $ prefix):
headingPath: Writing your first Django app, part 1 ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: We’ll assume you have Django installed already. You can tell Django is installed and which version by running the following command in a shell prompt (indicated by the $ prefix):

sourceRefs.fallbackText: $ python -m django --version
headingPath: Writing your first Django app, part 1 ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: $ python -m django --version

sourceRefs.fallbackText: ...\> py -m django --version
headingPath: Writing your first Django app, part 1 ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: ...\> py -m django --version

sourceRefs.fallbackText: If Django is installed, you should see the version of your installation. If it isn’t, you’ll get an error telling “No module named django”.
headingPath: Writing your first Django app, part 1 ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: If Django is installed, you should see the version of your installation. If it isn’t, you’ll get an error telling “No module named django”.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: This tutorial is written for Django 6.0, which supports Python 3.12 and later. If the Django version doesn’t match, you can refer to the tutorial for your version of Django by using the version switcher at the bottom right corner of this page, or update Django to the newest version. If you’re using an older version of Python, check What Python version can I
headingPath: Writing your first Django app, part 1 ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: This tutorial is written for Django 6.0, which supports Python 3.12 and later. If the Django version doesn’t match, you can refer to the tutorial for your version of Django by using the version switcher at the bottom rig

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Where to get help:
headingPath: Writing your first Django app, part 1 ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: Where to get help:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: If you’re having trouble going through this tutorial, please head over to the Getting Help section of the FAQ.
headingPath: Writing your first Django app, part 1 ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: If you’re having trouble going through this tutorial, please head over to the Getting Help section of the FAQ.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: If this is your first time using Django, you’ll have to take care of some initial setup. Namely, you’ll need to auto-generate some code that establishes a Django project – a collection of settings for an instance of Django, including database configuration, Django-specific options and application-specific settings.
headingPath: Writing your first Django app, part 1 ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: If this is your first time using Django, you’ll have to take care of some initial setup. Namely, you’ll need to auto-generate some code that establishes a Django project – a collection of settings for an instance of Djan

sourceRefs.fallbackText: From the command line, cd into a directory where you’d like to store your code and run the following command to bootstrap a new Django project:
headingPath: Writing your first Django app, part 1 ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: From the command line, cd into a directory where you’d like to store your code and run the following command to bootstrap a new Django project:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: $ django-admin startproject mysite djangotutorial
headingPath: Writing your first Django app, part 1 ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: $ django-admin startproject mysite djangotutorial

sourceRefs.fallbackText: ...\> django-admin startproject mysite djangotutorial
headingPath: Writing your first Django app, part 1 ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: ...\> django-admin startproject mysite djangotutorial

sourceRefs.fallbackText: This will create a directory djangotutorial with a project called mysite inside. The directory name doesn’t matter to Django; you can rename it to anything you like. If it didn’t work, see Problems running django-admin .
headingPath: Writing your first Django app, part 1 ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: This will create a directory djangotutorial with a project called mysite inside. The directory name doesn’t matter to Django; you can rename it to anything you like. If it didn’t work, see Problems running django-admin .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: You’ll need to avoid naming projects after built-in Python or Django components. In particular, this means you should avoid using names like django (which will conflict with Django itself) or test (which conflicts with a built-in Python package).
headingPath: Writing your first Django app, part 1 ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: You’ll need to avoid naming projects after built-in Python or Django components. In particular, this means you should avoid using names like django (which will conflict with Django itself) or test (which conflicts with a

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Let’s look at what startproject created:
headingPath: Writing your first Django app, part 1 ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: Let’s look at what startproject created:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: djangotutorial/ manage.py mysite/ __init__.py settings.py urls.py asgi.py wsgi.py
headingPath: Writing your first Django app, part 1 ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: djangotutorial/ manage.py mysite/ __init__.py settings.py urls.py asgi.py wsgi.py

sourceRefs.fallbackText: These files are:
headingPath: Writing your first Django app, part 1 ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: These files are:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: manage.py : A command-line utility that lets you interact with this Django project in various ways. You can read all the details about manage.py in django-admin and manage.py .
headingPath: Writing your first Django app, part 1 ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: manage.py : A command-line utility that lets you interact with this Django project in various ways. You can read all the details about manage.py in django-admin and manage.py .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: mysite/ : A directory that is the actual Python package for your project. Its name is the Python package name you’ll need to use to import anything inside it (e.g. mysite.urls ).
headingPath: Writing your first Django app, part 1 ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: mysite/ : A directory that is the actual Python package for your project. Its name is the Python package name you’ll need to use to import anything inside it (e.g. mysite.urls ).

sourceRefs.fallbackText: mysite/__init__.py : An empty file that tells Python that this directory should be considered a Python package. If you’re a Python beginner, read more about packages in the official Python docs.
headingPath: Writing your first Django app, part 1 ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: mysite/__init__.py : An empty file that tells Python that this directory should be considered a Python package. If you’re a Python beginner, read more about packages in the official Python docs.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: mysite/settings.py : Settings/configuration for this Django project. Django settings will tell you all about how settings work.
headingPath: Writing your first Django app, part 1 ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: mysite/settings.py : Settings/configuration for this Django project. Django settings will tell you all about how settings work.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: mysite/urls.py : The URL declarations for this Django project; a “table of contents” of your Django-powered site. You can read more about URLs in URL dispatcher .
headingPath: Writing your first Django app, part 1 ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: mysite/urls.py : The URL declarations for this Django project; a “table of contents” of your Django-powered site. You can read more about URLs in URL dispatcher .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: mysite/asgi.py : An entry-point for ASGI-compatible web servers to serve your project. See How to deploy with ASGI for more details.
headingPath: Writing your first Django app, part 1 ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: mysite/asgi.py : An entry-point for ASGI-compatible web servers to serve your project. See How to deploy with ASGI for more details.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: mysite/wsgi.py : An entry-point for WSGI-compatible web servers to serve your project. See How to deploy with WSGI for more details.
headingPath: Writing your first Django app, part 1 ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: mysite/wsgi.py : An entry-point for WSGI-compatible web servers to serve your project. See How to deploy with WSGI for more details.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Let’s verify your Django project works. Change into the djangotutorial directory, if you haven’t already, and run the following commands:
headingPath: Writing your first Django app, part 1 ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: Let’s verify your Django project works. Change into the djangotutorial directory, if you haven’t already, and run the following commands:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: $ python manage.py runserver
headingPath: Writing your first Django app, part 1 ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: $ python manage.py runserver

sourceRefs.fallbackText: ...\> py manage.py runserver
headingPath: Writing your first Django app, part 1 ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: ...\> py manage.py runserver

sourceRefs.fallbackText: You’ll see the following output on the command line:
headingPath: Writing your first Django app, part 1 ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: You’ll see the following output on the command line:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Performing system checks... System check identified no issues (0 silenced). You have unapplied migrations; your app may not work properly until they are applied. Run 'python manage.py migrate' to apply them. June 03, 2026 - 15:50:53 Django version 6.0, using settings 'mysite.settings' Starting development server at http://127.0.0.1:8000/ Quit the server with
headingPath: Writing your first Django app, part 1 ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: Performing system checks... System check identified no issues (0 silenced). You have unapplied migrations; your app may not work properly until they are applied. Run 'python manage.py migrate' to apply them. June 03, 202

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Ignore the warning about unapplied database migrations for now; we’ll deal with the database shortly.
headingPath: Writing your first Django app, part 1 ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: Ignore the warning about unapplied database migrations for now; we’ll deal with the database shortly.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Now that the server’s running, visit http://127.0.0.1:8000/ with your web browser. You’ll see a “Congratulations!” page, with a rocket taking off. It worked!
headingPath: Writing your first Django app, part 1 ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: Now that the server’s running, visit http://127.0.0.1:8000/ with your web browser. You’ll see a “Congratulations!” page, with a rocket taking off. It worked!

sourceRefs.fallbackText: You’ve started the Django development server, a lightweight web server written purely in Python. We’ve included this with Django so you can develop things rapidly, without having to deal with configuring a production server – such as Apache – until you’re ready for production.
headingPath: Writing your first Django app, part 1 ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: You’ve started the Django development server, a lightweight web server written purely in Python. We’ve included this with Django so you can develop things rapidly, without having to deal with configuring a production ser

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Now’s a good time to note: don’t use this server in anything resembling a production environment. It’s intended only for use while developing. (We’re in the business of making web frameworks, not web servers.)
headingPath: Writing your first Django app, part 1 ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: Now’s a good time to note: don’t use this server in anything resembling a production environment. It’s intended only for use while developing. (We’re in the business of making web frameworks, not web servers.)

sourceRefs.fallbackText: (To serve the site on a different port, see the runserver reference.)
headingPath: Writing your first Django app, part 1 ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: (To serve the site on a different port, see the runserver reference.)

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Automatic reloading of runserver
headingPath: Writing your first Django app, part 1 ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: Automatic reloading of runserver

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The development server automatically reloads Python code for each request as needed. You don’t need to restart the server for code changes to take effect. However, some actions like adding files don’t trigger a restart, so you’ll have to restart the server in these cases.
headingPath: Writing your first Django app, part 1 ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: The development server automatically reloads Python code for each request as needed. You don’t need to restart the server for code changes to take effect. However, some actions like adding files don’t trigger a restart,

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Now that your environment – a “project” – is set up, you’re set to start doing work.
headingPath: Writing your first Django app, part 1 ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: Now that your environment – a “project” – is set up, you’re set to start doing work.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Each application you write in Django consists of a Python package that follows a certain convention. Django comes with a utility that automatically generates the basic directory structure of an app, so you can focus on writing code rather than creating directories.
headingPath: Writing your first Django app, part 1 ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: Each application you write in Django consists of a Python package that follows a certain convention. Django comes with a utility that automatically generates the basic directory structure of an app, so you can focus on w

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Projects vs. apps
headingPath: Writing your first Django app, part 1 ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: Projects vs. apps

sourceRefs.fallbackText: What’s the difference between a project and an app? An app is a web application that does something – e.g., a blog system, a database of public records or a small poll app. A project is a collection of configuration and apps for a particular website. A project can contain multiple apps. An app can be in multiple projects.
headingPath: Writing your first Django app, part 1 ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: What’s the difference between a project and an app? An app is a web application that does something – e.g., a blog system, a database of public records or a small poll app. A project is a collection of configuration and

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Your apps can live anywhere in your Python path . In this tutorial, we’ll create our poll app inside the djangotutorial folder.
headingPath: Writing your first Django app, part 1 ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: Your apps can live anywhere in your Python path . In this tutorial, we’ll create our poll app inside the djangotutorial folder.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: To create your app, make sure you’re in the same directory as manage.py and type this command:
headingPath: Writing your first Django app, part 1 ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: To create your app, make sure you’re in the same directory as manage.py and type this command:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: $ python manage.py startapp polls
headingPath: Writing your first Django app, part 1 ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: $ python manage.py startapp polls

sourceRefs.fallbackText: ...\> py manage.py startapp polls
headingPath: Writing your first Django app, part 1 ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: ...\> py manage.py startapp polls

sourceRefs.fallbackText: That’ll create a directory polls , which is laid out like this:
headingPath: Writing your first Django app, part 1 ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: That’ll create a directory polls , which is laid out like this:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: polls/ __init__.py admin.py apps.py migrations/ __init__.py models.py tests.py views.py
headingPath: Writing your first Django app, part 1 ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: polls/ __init__.py admin.py apps.py migrations/ __init__.py models.py tests.py views.py

sourceRefs.fallbackText: This directory structure will house the poll application.
headingPath: Writing your first Django app, part 1 ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: This directory structure will house the poll application.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Let’s write the first view. Open the file polls/views.py and put the following Python code in it:
headingPath: Writing your first Django app, part 1 ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: Let’s write the first view. Open the file polls/views.py and put the following Python code in it:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: from django.http import HttpResponse def index ( request ): return HttpResponse ( "Hello, world. You're at the polls index." )
headingPath: Writing your first Django app, part 1 ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: from django.http import HttpResponse def index ( request ): return HttpResponse ( "Hello, world. You're at the polls index." )

sourceRefs.fallbackText: This is the most basic view possible in Django. To access it in a browser, we need to map it to a URL - and for this we need to define a URL configuration, or “URLconf” for short. These URL configurations are defined inside each Django app, and they are Python files named urls.py .
headingPath: Writing your first Django app, part 1 ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: This is the most basic view possible in Django. To access it in a browser, we need to map it to a URL - and for this we need to define a URL configuration, or “URLconf” for short. These URL configurations are defined ins

sourceRefs.fallbackText: To define a URLconf for the polls app, create a file polls/urls.py with the following content:
headingPath: Writing your first Django app, part 1 ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: To define a URLconf for the polls app, create a file polls/urls.py with the following content:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: from django.urls import path from . import views urlpatterns = [ path ( "" , views . index , name = "index" ), ]
headingPath: Writing your first Django app, part 1 ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: from django.urls import path from . import views urlpatterns = [ path ( "" , views . index , name = "index" ), ]

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Your app directory should now look like:
headingPath: Writing your first Django app, part 1 ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: Your app directory should now look like:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: polls/ __init__.py admin.py apps.py migrations/ __init__.py models.py tests.py urls.py views.py
headingPath: Writing your first Django app, part 1 ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: polls/ __init__.py admin.py apps.py migrations/ __init__.py models.py tests.py urls.py views.py

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The next step is to configure the root URLconf in the mysite project to include the URLconf defined in polls.urls . To do this, add an import for django.urls.include in mysite/urls.py and insert an include() in the urlpatterns list, so you have:
headingPath: Writing your first Django app, part 1 ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: The next step is to configure the root URLconf in the mysite project to include the URLconf defined in polls.urls . To do this, add an import for django.urls.include in mysite/urls.py and insert an include() in the urlpa

sourceRefs.fallbackText: from django.contrib import admin from django.urls import include , path urlpatterns = [ path ( "polls/" , include ( "polls.urls" )), path ( "admin/" , admin . site . urls ), ]
headingPath: Writing your first Django app, part 1 ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: from django.contrib import admin from django.urls import include , path urlpatterns = [ path ( "polls/" , include ( "polls.urls" )), path ( "admin/" , admin . site . urls ), ]

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The path() function expects at least two arguments: route and view . The include() function allows referencing other URLconfs. Whenever Django encounters include() , it chops off whatever part of the URL matched up to that point and sends the remaining string to the included URLconf for further processing.
headingPath: Writing your first Django app, part 1 ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: The path() function expects at least two arguments: route and view . The include() function allows referencing other URLconfs. Whenever Django encounters include() , it chops off whatever part of the URL matched up to th

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The idea behind include() is to make it easy to plug-and-play URLs. Since polls are in their own URLconf ( polls/urls.py ), they can be placed under “/polls/”, or under “/fun_polls/”, or under “/content/polls/”, or any other path root, and the app will still work.
headingPath: Writing your first Django app, part 1 ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: The idea behind include() is to make it easy to plug-and-play URLs. Since polls are in their own URLconf ( polls/urls.py ), they can be placed under “/polls/”, or under “/fun_polls/”, or under “/content/polls/”, or any o

sourceRefs.fallbackText: When to use include()
headingPath: Writing your first Django app, part 1 ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: When to use include()

sourceRefs.fallbackText: You should always use include() when you include other URL patterns. The only exception is admin.site.urls , which is a pre-built URLconf provided by Django for the default admin site.
headingPath: Writing your first Django app, part 1 ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: You should always use include() when you include other URL patterns. The only exception is admin.site.urls , which is a pre-built URLconf provided by Django for the default admin site.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: You have now wired an index view into the URLconf. Verify it’s working with the following command:
headingPath: Writing your first Django app, part 1 ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: You have now wired an index view into the URLconf. Verify it’s working with the following command:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: $ python manage.py runserver
headingPath: Writing your first Django app, part 1 ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: $ python manage.py runserver

sourceRefs.fallbackText: ...\> py manage.py runserver
headingPath: Writing your first Django app, part 1 ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: ...\> py manage.py runserver

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Go to http://localhost:8000/polls/ in your browser, and you should see the text “ Hello, world. You’re at the polls index. ”, which you defined in the index view.
headingPath: Writing your first Django app, part 1 ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: Go to http://localhost:8000/polls/ in your browser, and you should see the text “ Hello, world. You’re at the polls index. ”, which you defined in the index view.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: If you get an error page here, check that you’re going to http://localhost:8000/polls/ and not http://localhost:8000/ .
headingPath: Writing your first Django app, part 1 ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: If you get an error page here, check that you’re going to http://localhost:8000/polls/ and not http://localhost:8000/ .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: When you’re comfortable with the basic request and response flow, read part 2 of this tutorial to start working with the database.
headingPath: Writing your first Django app, part 1 ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: When you’re comfortable with the basic request and response flow, read part 2 of this tutorial to start working with the database.
